title: World History, Constitutional Civics & Economics
subject: History & Social Studies
description: Examine world historical revolutions, constitutional governance, civil rights, economic trade systems, and community resilience.
learningObjectives: Analyze key principles of constitutional governance, separation of powers, and individual rights.
Understand supply and demand, inflation, and trade economics.
Trace major turning points in modern world history.

lessons.title: Constitutional Governance & Separation of Powers
lessons.content: Constitutional governance limits state power through rule of law, protecting fundamental individual liberties. Separation of powers distributes authority across Legislative, Executive, and Judicial branches to prevent tyranny.

lessons.title: Economic Principles: Scarcity & Supply/Demand
lessons.content: Economics is the study of resource allocation under conditions of scarcity. Supply represents how much of a good is available; Demand represents consumer desire to purchase it. Price equilibrium occurs where supply equals demand.

flashcards.definition: Division of government authority among distinct branches to prevent concentration of power.
The fundamental economic problem of having unlimited wants in a world of limited resources.
The principle that all people and institutions are subject to and accountable to law that is fairly enforced.

questions.question: Which principle prevents any single branch of government from acquiring absolute control?
options: Separation of Powers & Checks and Balances

questions.question: What happens to the price of a scarce resource when demand increases while supply remains constant?

practicalChallenge.title: Constitutional Analysis & Bill of Rights Review
practicalChallenge.instruction: Read the Bill of Rights or Canadian Charter of Rights and Freedoms. Choose 2 key rights and write a 1-page summary explaining why they are critical for a free society!
